learning_objectives: Understand how the Waterfall model works and when to use it

images_in_block.alt: Diagram of Waterfall phases flowing top to bottom

context.source_html:
Waterfall Model

Step-by-step way of developing software where each phase must be completed before moving to the next.

  • Requirement Analysis
  • Design
  • Development
  • Testing
  • Deployment
  • Maintenance

Best suited when: requirements are clear and fixed; project is small or simple; customer won't change their mind often.

Advantages: Simple to understand and use; every phase has clear documentation. Disadvantages: No feedback until the end; hard to go back and fix earlier steps; costly to fix late mistakes.

output.content_html:
Understanding the Waterfall Model

Model 1 of 3 — a one-direction, sequential approach

The Waterfall Model is a step-by-step way of building software where each phase must be fully completed before the next one begins. Work moves in one direction through the phases — once a phase is done, the team moves on to the next one.

How it works

The phases run in a fixed order — requirement analysis, then design, development, testing, deployment, and finally maintenance. Everything is planned at the start, and the team passes through each stage exactly once.

When to use it

Best suited when:

  • Requirements are clear and fixed from the start
  • The project is small or simple

Advantages & disadvantages

Advantages

  • Simple to understand, with clear documentation at every phase

Disadvantages

  • No feedback until the very end, so fixing late mistakes is costly

image_decisions.reason: The sequential phase flow is the core concept of the block and is best shown as an animated diagram.

examples.notes: Strong example. Leads with the concept then explains it in connected prose, staying strictly within the source: every fact (phases, when-to-use, pros/cons) traces to the source, and because the source uses no analogy, none is invented (the wording reworks the source's own 'one phase before the next' description). Uses only house-style components inside .main-content, and places the animation marker at the natural 'how it works' point rather than dumping the source's phase bullets.

context.block_title: Why Different Projects Need Different Models
learning_objectives: Understand why different projects need different development approaches
context.source_html:
Why Not One SDLC for All?

Different software projects have different needs. A small app and a large banking system cannot be built the same way.

What are Software Development Models?

Software development models are different ways of arranging the phases of the SDLC to fit the needs of a project. The three most commonly used are the Waterfall Model, the Agile Model, and the V-Model.

output.content_html:
Why Different Projects Need Different Models

One process does not fit every project

Not every software project should be built the same way. A small app and a large banking system have very different needs, so the same approach cannot suit both.

That is why we use software development models — different ways of arranging the phases of the Software Development Life Cycle (SDLC) to fit a project's needs. In this session we look at the three most commonly used: the Waterfall Model, the Agile Model, and the V-Model.

examples.notes: Merges the two short intro sections into one cohesive block and motivates the topic before naming the models, using only what the source states (the small-app vs banking-system contrast). The source offers no analogy, so none is invented. No images in the source, so image_decisions is correctly empty.

learning_objectives: Understand the V-Model and when to use it

images_in_block.alt: V-shaped diagram mapping development phases to testing phases

context.source_html:
V-Model

V-Model stands for Verification and Validation Model. It is an extension of Waterfall with a testing phase mapped to every development phase.

Best suited for projects where requirements are clear and quality is critical, such as medical or defence software.

output.content_html:
Understanding the V-Model

Model 3 of 3 — testing planned alongside every step

The V-Model stands for Verification and Validation Model. It is an extension of the Waterfall Model — it keeps the same one-direction, sequential flow, but pairs a matching testing phase with every development phase.

If Waterfall is a straight line top to bottom, the V-Model bends that line into a V: the left side goes down through the development phases, and the right side comes back up through the test that verifies each one. So a test is planned the moment a phase is designed, not left until the end.

When to use it

Best suited when:

  • Requirements are clear and unlikely to change
  • Quality is critical, such as medical or defence software

image_decisions.reason: The V-shape that maps each development phase to a test phase is the defining idea and benefits from an animated diagram.

examples.notes: Accurately frames the V-Model as an extension of Waterfall (a fact in the source) and explains the V-shape with a connecting analogy to the previously taught Waterfall block. Keeps the when-to-use examples (medical/defence) straight from the source without embellishing. Animation marker sits right after the explanation of the shape.
